summary: Jeff Atwood argues that Office 2007's Ribbon interface represents a revolutionary departure from the traditional WIMP (Windows, Icons, Menus, Pointing device) GUI paradigm that has dominated desktop software. He contends that by eliminating the main menu and de-emphasizing windows, Microsoft has essentially reduced the interface to just Icons and Pointing devices—the same model that powers web browsers. Atwood sees this as significant because Office traditionally sets UI trends that the rest of the industry follows, evidenced by IE7 and Vista already hiding their menus by default. He praises Microsoft for taking such a bold risk with their flagship product and declares it the first version of Office worth upgrading to.
key_points: Office 2007's Ribbon interface signals the death of the main menu as a fundamental GUI metaphor
The new interface reduces WIMP to just 'IP'—Icons and Pointing devices—mirroring web browser design
Web browsers have proven that users adapt more readily to simpler interfaces without traditional menus and windows
Microsoft's willingness to overhaul their flagship product's UI demonstrates unusual courage, especially compared to Vista's conservative approach
Where Office goes with UI conventions, the rest of the software industry tends to follow
money_quotes: The new Office 2007 user interface is one of the most innovative things to come out of Redmond in years.
It's nothing less than the death of the main menu as a keystone GUI metaphor.
If Office 2007 drops the W and M from WIMP, what are we left with? IP. Icons and Pointing Devices.
The world's most popular GUI environment, the web browser, has no Menus or Windows.
People seem to adapt to the web much more readily than traditional WIMP apps.
It's impressive that Microsoft was willing to make such a large change to their flagship application.
It's the first version of Office worth upgrading to.
key_insight: Office 2007's abandonment of traditional menus aligns desktop software with the simpler, more intuitive interface model that web browsers have proven users prefer.
